dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for possible seizure at 798 Owen Ave.
Hi, I'm Drew, a paramedic with EMS. What made you call 911 today?
A 60-year-old man had a seizure about 30 minutes ago. He hit his head.
Patient is unresponsive but breathing. Was there any speech or movement before the seizure?
He didn't respond to verbal stimuli but opened his eyes spontaneously at one point.
Yes, he seemed to open his eyes when his name was called.
Mr. Taylor can lift his arms slightly but shows minimal coordination. No full response to commands.
Airway is patent and no immediate obstruction noted.
Respiration is at 20 per minute, no cyanosis observed.
Pulse is 97, strong and regular. Blood pressure is high at 193 over 77.
Is there any prior history of seizures or neurological issues?
He was standing in the bathroom when he suddenly stiffened, fell, and hit his head.
First seizure lasted about 3 to 5 minutes, and he was confused for a while after.
Did he stop breathing or experience cyanosis?
No, but he was weaker and started a second seizure about 2 minutes after the first one.
Pupils are pinpoint and sluggish. Any pain in your head or neck, Mr. Taylor?
Pinpoint pupils might suggest seizures, medication use, or head trauma.
Respirations currently stable at 20 per minute.
Pulse remains at 97 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 193 over 77 and later recheck showed 163 over 88.
Blood glucose initially at 123, later readings showed zero.
Oxygen saturation is at 100 percent, then 98 percent later. Current spo2 is 97%.
Based on presentation, initiating medical-seizure protocol 3-12 as instructed.
Starting four-lead ECG monitoring now.
Four-lead now in place. Rhythm is currently normal.
Administering Midazolam 5mg via intramuscular into the upper right deltoid.
EKG changed to sinus rhythm post-Midazolam.
Respiration rate still 20.
Pulse is still 97 and stable.
Blood pressure is now 173 over 100 mmHg, then later 163 over 88.
IV started in the left hand using a 20G needle with saline lock.
IV is in good place, no issues noted.
Naloxone 0.5mg administered IV with no response.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia.
Completed 12-lead EKG with findings of possible inferior infarct and sinus tachycardia.
Pulse is now 109, respiration is 21, and BP is 146/94. A previous reading showed 105.
Oxygen saturation is at 97 percent now.
Preparing to transport to [CITY] Emergency Department.
Patient secured on the stretcher using all restraints. Positioning is semi-fowler.
Patient moved to outside stretcher. Positioning semi-fowler as ordered; airway remains patent on transport.
IV placed in an extremity vein per protocol.
Capnography activated and stable. Monitoring adjusted before transport.
Latest blood glucose was at 0 on reassessment.
Respiration of 22 occurred following Midazolam use, no signs of airway compromise.
Recheck IV site and EKG readings-no new findings or changes in tone or blood pressure.
Confirm no pain reported for all assessments. GCS was E4V1M5 on scene.
Securing stretcher for movement.
All lines and leads in place for transit.
Patient had another seizure but was stabilized via medication.
Final GCS on scene at 10 (E4V1M5).
Breaking down initial kit status and get ready for sidewalk wheel transfer.
No signs of neurologic regression or breathing compromise-ready for transport handover.
Midazolam given as "versed" per protocol.
Naloxone administered and ongoing reassessment shows no clinical effect.
No significant pain reported by the patient on multiple assessments. Pain status was clear.
Pulse shows a progression from 97 to 109 with an intermediate reading of 105 acknowledged.
All pre-transit assessments complete, pulse, SpO2, and EKG stable.
IV secured and continuous monitoring set.
Capnography showing proper waveform, nothing abnormal.
All per protocols, no deviations from seizure protocol initiated.
Patient moved to outside stretcher. Positioning semi-fowler as ordered; airway remains patent on transport.
All interventions verified by partner and myself independently.
Administration of Midazolam again confirmed as "versed."
Capnography signal remains strong with transport wheels engaged.
Blood pressure at 146/94; final pulse at 109 with sinus tachycardia.
Oxygen saturation remains at 97%; no requirement for supplemental oxygen.
All team standing by for exit transfer to [COMPANY_NAME].
No further medications or procedures required for now.
Final review of all documentation complete. No omissions or inaccuracies noted.
All lead wires, IV, and capnography cables re-checked. Transporting with all function intact.
Full field EKG in possession, ready for hard copy analysis if required.
Patient showed no further seizure activity after last medication.
Patient had two seizure events but is resting now with close observation.
No further trend changes observed. Pain status remains at zero for full assessment cycle.
No ongoing immediate threats to life. All vitals recorded as per online entries.
Ready to begin transfer to stretcher outside the premises.
No resistance in patient movement. Solid assist with two EMS on board.
Final check on Midazolam and Naloxone-no blockage issue noted.
Naloxone is part of standing protocol for unknown cause seizure per incident.
IV fluid status good to go and has remained patent during titration.
All interventions documented and logged as per online EMS database.
Infarct pattern noted on 12-lead, PVG open, airway healthy.
Pain profiling excluded in all reassessments; null reported for duration.
Poison effects not in play. Drugs checked and not involved in this case.
Facilitating non-agitated reposition to transport unit for ED transfer.
Consequences low. Unresponsive initially, but later responsive and stable.
Transport safety confirmed at final point before exit pathway.
All interventions verified by partner and myself independently.
SpO2 of 97% maintained through full transport set-up and reassessment.
Transporting with semi-fowler positioning, IV in line, all monitors alive, go mode.
Final check of IV site completed. No infiltration, pressure, or compromise.
Noted: 105 pulse reading was recorded as per earlier cycles.
Exit secured. Stretcher awaiting ramp deployment.
No secondary interventions required at this point. Stable per all signs.
We'll be en route with no indication of decline. Handing over in semi-fowler for ED team.
Final outtake vitals confirm stability and no acute change. All stays consistent.
Wheelchair transfer ongoing with no interruption in monitoring during mobility.
Oxygen saturation stable; no escalation needed in current state of care.
Transporting with zero reported pain on recent and prior assessment.
Full protocol completed. [COMPANY_NAME] is the intended destination for clearance.
